data.title: Frontier Airlines Remains A 'Hold' As Strategy Shift Has Yet To Crystalize
data.description:
Frontier Group Holdings stock (ULCC) has climbed 26.2% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s gain. The stock price now slightly exceeds the $5.25 price target I set, providing a good moment to look at what lies ahead for Frontier Airlines and update my price target.

Wall Street Expectations For Frontier Airlines In Q4 2025

Analysts are expecting Frontier Airlines to report revenues between $946.5 million and $988.9 million, with a midpoint estimate of $971.4 million, indicating 3.1% lower sales compared to a year ago. We do note that the company does not have a strong record of beating expectations, as it has missed estimates three quarters consecutively, and over the past 12 quarters, it beat estimates only three times.

Earnings per share estimates range between $0.10 and $0.18, with a midpoint estimate of $0.14, indicating a 39% decline year-on-year. Luckily, on earnings per share, the track record is substantially better with two misses over the past 12 quarters.

In recent months, revenue estimates for the fourth quarter have barely changed, with a 0.6% downward revision over the past month and a 2.75% downward revision over the past three months. Five analysts revised estimates down, while three analysts revised estimates upward. EPS estimates, however, substantially improved with a 48% improvement over the past three months and a 66.4% improvement over the past month. Revisions of analysts were split equally.

Strategic Shifts Drive Revisions In Favor Of Frontier Airlines

The upward revisions for Frontier Airlines are likely driven by changes that the low-cost carrier is making as well as changes in the competitive landscape. Spirit Airlines has exited 36 overlapping routes and reduced capacity by 30% on another 41 routes, driving a 2% competitive capacity reduction in the fourth quarter with a potentially even bigger impact on competitive capacity decline into 2026. Furthermore, the company is seeing more loyalty revenues, and in the spring of 2026, the company is launching first class. Low-cost carriers have seen substantial pressure on operations as inflation and pilot shortages inflated costs, and that led to Spirit Airlines substantially downsizing and other low-cost airlines rethinking their cabin segmentation. That should drive performance in the fourth quarter and into 2026, with full maturity for first-class introduction expected in roughly three years.

ULCC Stock Remains A 'Hold'

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Frontier Airlines is a bit of a complex company to value since the company has no credible median EV/EBITDA, given that it became a public company during the pandemic. The current EV/BITDA of 8.6x shows that at current prices the stock is fully valued for the expected earnings in 2025. For 2026 and beyond, I am applying a 6.6x EV/EBITDA, which is one unit lower than the 7.6x EV/EBITDA for profitable peers. I am applying that discount given that at present it is uncertain how the company will finance new aircraft acquisitions, including pre-delivery payments. My assumption currently is that the company will engage in sale-and-lease-back transactions combined with adding debt. As long as the company does not have a positive operating cash flow, I do believe it is justified to apply a discount and maintain a hold rating even though potential upside is 46%. The upside, in my view, is too fragile and uncertain given the company’s high capital needs, and I also believe that the company has to rethink its contracted fleet additions. In some ways, Frontier is facing a dilemma that many airlines face, where they are burning cash and adding new airplanes to amortize costs and turn positive on operating cash flow.

If we look at the financial performance, the expectation is that competitive capacity declines and a reshaping strategy for Frontier Airlines, including a first-class product and growth in loyalty revenues, will result in a substantial EBITDA margin recovery. Operating cash performance, however, is expected to only be positive by 2027, with current fleet additions being financed through sale-and-lease-back transactions as well as debt. For ultra-low-cost carriers, I do believe net-debt leverage should be in the 3x-5x range. Frontier Airlines is expected to achieve that in 2026, but a lot of the appeal for the company is going to hinge on its ability to actually turn a positive operating cash flow.

Conclusion: Frontier Airlines Fleet Additions Provide Pressure

The fleet planning of Frontier Airlines is a bit of a double-edged sword. On one hand, the company seems to require additional scale to drive down unit costs and achieve a positive operating cash flow. On the other hand, that requires substantial capital needs that currently can only be covered through net debt additions and engaging in sale-and-lease-back transactions. So, higher stock prices are possible, but I maintain a "Hold" rating as I believe we first need to see substantial signs that Frontier’s strategy is working.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace: Buy This Speculative Bet Focused On Execution
data.description:
Thesis

I view Firefly Aerospace Inc. (FLY) as a speculative Buy.

Below I will analyze the company with my main focus on its operational progress and its ability to execute missions consistently in space.

The value comes from building a real business around the technology that can generate regular revenue.

The stock recently entered Russell 2000 Index (RTY). This improved its visibility and helped, through the increased liquidity the stabilization of the shareholder base.

Improving the pace of missions and strengthening the presence in government and defense programs can change the image of the company.

This investment is for those who are attracted by volatility and are willing to wait for operational maturity.

Company Overview

Firefly is a company focused on launching payloads into space.

These payloads are typically small to medium sized and include satellites, scientific equipment or missions for government agencies.

The Rocket "ALPHA" is one of the basic tools of the company and is designed for a specific launch and not for very heavy payloads.

This means that Firefly is not trying to compete the biggest players of the sector but wants to serve a specific part of the market.

This choice reduces the need for very large investments and allows for a more focused commercial strategy while the Blue Ghost program brings Firefly into missions related to the Moon.

This sector is gradually gaining importance, as government organizations invest in permanent presence and infrastructure.

IPO and Market Sentiment

Firefly went public at a time when investors were showing great interest in companies operating in space.

There was a lot of excitement around the so called space economy and many investors believed that companies in the sector could grow quickly.

This resulted in high initial valuations ($45 price) and very optimistic expectations.

In practice running a space company proved to be more difficult and slower than the market had expected.

Firefly dealt with technical problems and delays in its missions which consequently affected its image while the pace of execution was not consistent. This led to disappointment and to a sharp drop in the stock after the listing.

This development shows the difference between vision and reality.

It is a common phenomenon in new technology companies where plans take time to be properly implemented.

In the case of Firefly, the company did not change course or abandon its investments to calm the market.

Instead, it continued to invest in facilities, personnel and process improvements.

This as i see it, is a positive sign, because it shows that management is focused on building the right foundation and not just supporting the stock in the short term.

News Flow and Strategic Direction

Recently, Firefly’s management has shown that it has shifted priorities and is paying more attention to how the company actually works.

Its focus is now on proper organization, better quality control and improving daily operations and this is important as mistakes in space sector are costly. For me, the key issue is getting the Alpha rocket back on a stable mission schedule because one successful launch is not enough.

That is valuable is that the company can perform missions over and over again, without major delays.

The inclusion of the stock in the Russell 2000 index helps Firefly become better known to institutional investors and increases the liquidity of the stock.

This doesn’t change the company’s fundamentals but it reduces volatility and make the stock more stable.

At the same time, Firefly’s increasing involvement in defense-related projects makes practical sense.

Defense contracts tend to last longer and offer better revenue visibility.

Financials

Q32025 results show that Firefly can generate revenue when missions are active and run without delays.

Revenue increased by 38% vs last year, which in my view, show that customers want the company's services.

This is easier to understand when you look at the business model. Launches are not evenly distributed throughout the year and revenue tends to be concentrated in specific quarters.

For this reason, the strong quarterly volatility does not particularly concern me as I consider this normal for a space company that is not yet operating at a steady, repeatable pace.

Losses remain high and the company continues to burn capital, which is entirely expected for a space technology company in its growth stage. I do not evaluate Firefly based on EPS or short-term profitability. The key metric i watch is liquidity and the time it gives management to improve operational execution.

The company almost $1 billion in cash, which gives it a strong safety buffer. This gives the company the time to fix issues, stabilize operations and turn growing demand into more predictable income.

This point is very critical to the whole investment evaluation as it distinguishes Firefly from smaller firms with less financial flexibility.

Valuation

The stock trades at $30 per share.

Company reports negative earnings and high cash consumption. This means that traditional metrics like P/E are not useful shifting the spotlight to enterprise value to revenue and operational development scenarios.

The company has strong liquidity with close to $1 billion in cash. This minimizes the funding risk and gives the company time to improve execution. Its backlog of $1.3 billion gives visibility into future demand only if missions are delivered as planned.

Now let's analyze three scenarios.

In the bear case scenario, execution is unstable, delays continue and trust is not rebuilt. The market is likely to keep a high risk premium. In this scenario a share price near $12 is expected (60% below current levels) as there will be loss of confidence in the company’s ability to mature operationally.

In the base case scenario where launches become more stable but not fully repeatable, a price of $30 to $35 or flat to about a 17% increase, seems reasonable.

In the bull case scenario, with stable and repeatable launches, stronger defense exposure and clearer revenue trends, the stock could be rerated toward $80 (>165% higher than today) as Firefly is seen as a real operating business rather than just a technology bet.

Firefly is a high risk company but with very high potential reward.

For this reason, the stock is a speculative buy suitable only for investors who can handle volatility.

On Nasdaq, FLY shows its aggressive nature. In less than 5 months of trading since its listing (in August 2025), it has already suffered a massive 62% loss and an extraordinary recovery of +79%.

Technically, the range of $31.90 to $32.50 is the main resistance after the big gap from the 21% drop on September 30.

The buy signal from $19 is still valid and the stock’s inclusion in the Russell may be helping to keep buying interest alive.

Risks

As i see it, the most significant risk is the technical reliability of the missions, as success in the space industry requires a high degree of precision and zero tolerance for errors.

A single failed mission can damage the company’s reputation, delay its launch schedule and weaken trust among existing and potential customers.

Another risk is the ability to keep a steady launch schedule. If you ask me irregular execution creates revenue volatility, reduces predictability and makes long-term planning harder for both management and investors.

Firefly is still using cash, which is normal but it must keep enough liquidity.

Another weakness is that the company also depends on a limited number of programs and customers. This makes it more exposed to cancellations, delays or budget cuts (especially in government contracts).

Because the valuation already assumes future success, the stock can be extremely volatile to negative news.

Competition in the space industry is intense. There are new and existing competitors that want the market shares with lower cost.

Conclusion

In short, Firefly Aerospace is a high-risk investment that needs strong and steady execution in a tough industry.

The addition of the stock to Russell 2000 increases visibility and liquidity. This could reduce extreme volatility in the long term.

This does not change the fundamentals but it supports the investment case. The stock is perfect for investors who love risk and are attracted by high volatile stocks.

Firefly is a speculative Buy based on execution progress and not short-term results.

data.title: Quince Therapeutics: Good Potential In Orphan Disease In Q1 With Platform Potential
data.description:
Thesis

Quince Therapeutics (QNCX) is doing a Phase 3 trial in Ataxia-Telangiectasia [A-T], an orphan disease without known treatment affecting 10,000 patients worldwide. Its Phase 3 trial called NEAT addresses issues related to failure in the past, and historical causes for failures in similar diseases. I discuss these below.

If successful, it may become the first approved treatment for A-T worldwide, and the company’s therapy could be applied in other diseases as well.

The Phase 3 readout is therefore probably around the corner. Last patient last visit took place in December 2025 and results are due mid-Q1 2026, which I assume could be next month.

The A-T market is over $1 billion, which may lead to significant upside in case of success, irrespective of eDSP’s further platform play potential.

Quince Therapeutics

eDSP in Ataxia-Telangiectasia: earlier results

Quince Therapeutics’ treatment candidate eDSP for A-T adds dexamethasone to a patient’s red blood cells, allowing for slow release of that drug contrary to traditional toxic administration of corticosteroids.

Quince acquired eDSP from Erydel some years ago, after Erydel had failed a 6-month Phase 3 trial A-T called ATTeST, which was high-profile and the largest clinical study ever run in A-T. The primary reason that was quoted for the failure of that trial was lack of symptom progression in patients 10 years and above. Lack of symptom progression is a typical cause for trial failures in ataxias.

A statistically significant result was achieved in the 6-9 year age group receiving the high dose, showing a 4.8-point difference on the ICARS rating scale (p=0.0016).

Placebo patients aged 6-9 progressed much faster than older patients, with a 4.3 point difference between both groups.

That result aligns with positive results from an earlier 6-month Phase 2 trial in A-T in 21 patients, which also showed a 4-point difference on ICARS, with a 12.75 point improvement in less neurologically impaired patients.

In the extension study of that trial, ICARS scores for patients on drug further improved while patients no longer on drug worsened or remained stable.

NEAT trial

Quince is currently finalizing its randomized placebo-controlled NEAT trial in 105 patients, for which enrollment started in 2024 and was complete in July 2025. The last patient completed his last visit on December 15, 2025. Results are awaited mid-Q1 2026, which should in principle be next month.

NEAT is essentially a better-designed ATTeST trial in order to achieve a statistically significant results in the primary analysis population, i.e. the 6-9 year old age group. I think chances are very high that positive results could be achieved. The trial at least seems to be a clear improvement compared to the ATTeST trial, which as such did not achieve a bad result, just one that was not statistically significant – yet superior to placebo – in the entire patient population.

Targeted patient population with highest disease progression

The 6-9 year old age group is the primary analysis population for this trial. Studies, both internally as externally, show that symptom progression is highest in the 6-9 year-old age group, and then considerably slows down.

This could explain the earlier ATTeST trial failure, and is a considerable improvement to trial design in my eyes.

Further trial improvements

Historical failures in ataxia, generally orphan diseases, are, among others, related to variability of symptom progression across patients, rare disease recruitment challenges and endpoint insensitivity. With the trial focusing on 6-9 year old patients and having been fully enrolled, the first two potential issues seem to be tackled.

Additionally, the NEAT trial uses RmICARS, as requested by the FDA under a Special Protocol Assessment, as its primary endpoint. RmICARS focuses on domains that were more sensitive to change in the ATTeST trial.

Changes on ICARS, mICARS and RmICARS between the ages 6 and 10 from the ATTeST trial reveal scoring differences of respectively +19%, +34% and +46%, indicating highest sensitivity for RmICARs in the 6-9 year age group which forms the primary analysis population of the NEAT trial. During its Investor Day, Quince stated that the RmICARS is probably the most sensitive endpoint, and I see this confirmed by a patient-reported comparison of different scales indicating the RmICARS is equally good or better at tracking differences in disease progression.

The NEAT trial incorporates further trial modifications which I interpret as positive.

  • The high dose, which had better results in the ATTeST trial, is the only one used in the NEAT trial.
  • Trial powering is different; NEAT is 90% powered vs. 80% usually to detect a 2.4 difference on RmICARS, with a 3-point confidence interval for patients on treatment, and 3.7 points for placebo, powering the study for a delay in progression of disease of about 24%.
  • The NEAT trial has a 4% discontinuation rate, vs. 24% for the ATTeST trial, which could be related to the lack of Covid-related issues;
  • NEAT only has 4% missing data vs. 26% for ATTeST, which is good as NEAT’s powering assumptions were based on the ATTeST’s trial’s missing data.
  • Rollover rates in the extension study, which were already high in the ATTeST trial [96%], are now 99%.

One further difference to be noted is that the extension trial data collected from the ATTeST trial, including a good safety profile, is now available. That OLE ran from 2018 to 2022, allowing Quince to collect data of about 70 patients that were on therapy for over three years. The high rollover and adherence rate in both trials indicates that the therapy may be having an effect on patients.

Looking at those differences, I think NEAT may have successfully tackled the obstacles that impeded ATTeST’s success.

One could argue that the NEAT trial does not have biomarkers progression as endpoint, and in that sense, the trial could still have been better designed. For example, in a Phase 2a/b trial testing triheptanoin in A-T, NfL was used as a biomarker, yielding good results. In light of the SPA which should allow the FDA to approve eDSP on the basis of one pivotal trial, I do not see that point of particular concern.

Estimating chances of success

The ATTeST trial included 176 patients in the intent-to-treat population. Randomization across high-dose, low-dose and placebo was spread evenly across the entire mITT population. A total of 33 patients aged 6-9 were on the high dose.

The mITT 6-9 year old age group had a 4.8 difference in scoring on the mICARS rating scale, with a p-value of 0.0016, which means high statistical significance.

The difference between ICARS and RmICARS is not only the scoring (a total of 100 points versus a 29-point total), but also where the scoring most relates to. Quince presented a poster in that respect, showing among others:

  • That progression from age 6-10 was +19% on ICARS, +34% on mICARS and +46% on RmICARS;
  • The ICARS subcomponents which increased more than 25% in that age group were walking [100%], standing capacity [40%] and sitting [27%], which relate to posture and gait;
  • That RmICARS captures most progression in that age group – note that total points on RmICARS are less than one third of total scores on ICARS, meaning that an average of ~18 points relates to about 60%of the entire scoring on that scale, whereas an average of about ~44 relates to less than half of total scoring on ICARS;

No age-related trends were seen on speech, oculomotor and kinetic function.

The RmICARS rating scale focuses exactly on those areas, with most of the points attributed to posture and gait disturbance, and much less to kinetic function, and without any rating for oculomotor disorders.

Focusing on the areas where progression is highest should positively influence Cohen’s D/effect size.

Based on the ATTeST trial, if one were to estimate significance on RmICARS for the 6-9 year old age group, statistical significance would be met with a p-value of 0.009, which is again highly statistically significant (the cut-off is p < 0.05).

In summary, the RmICARS rating scale tracks disease progression focused on 6-9 year old patients. When, in 33 patients, the RmICARS rating scale was used, this led to a p-value of 0.009.

In comparison, the current NEAT study’s primary analysis population, i.e. the 6-9 year-old patients, comprises 83 patients which, assuming half are on placebo, is 26% more than the same group of patients in the ATTeST trial. Assuming 4% missing data would bring that population down to about 80. NEAT is powered at 90% to detect a 2.4 point difference, but its powering assumptions for missing data were based on ATTeST’s 26% whereas NEAT only has 4% missing data. Furthermore, NEAT trial has a 4% discontinuation rate, vs. 24% for the ATTeST trial.

A larger N should directly affect the standard error and chances of statistical precision. The effective sample size is much higher, which I believe may affect statistical precision. Missing data influences variances and weakens effective sample size. In ATTeST, with 32 patients on the high dose and 24% missing data, effective sample size was probably closer to 24 patients. In NEAT, taking into account 4% missing data, we are probably looking at about a 40-patient effective sample size on therapy (and about 80 patients in the primary analysis population). In that sense, the difference of 4% [NEAT] versus 24% [ATTeST] may have a significant influence. Combining both, I think the likelihood of a false-negative result has been reduced by 66%.

Standard error should be lower than in the ATTeST trial, as it is inversely proportional to the square root of the sample size. Statistical precision should increase by 29%, and variance should decrease by 40%.

All of these also influence the minimum scoring effect needed for statistical significance. As mentioned above, NEAT was powered for a 2.4-point scoring difference on RmICARS. My estimate is that a 1.45-point difference on RmICARS would be the floor for statistical significance, allowing NEAT to substantially underperform results shown in ATTeST. That assumes a target difference of 2.4 points, a p-value 0.05, 90% statistical power, and Z-scores of respectively 1.96 (Alpha) and 1.282 (Beta).

From that perspective, my assumption would be that NEAT, in practice, is a highly powered trial to confirm an effect that was already previously seen to be highly statistically significant.

Safety

I do not see any concerns from a safety and tolerability perspective. eDSP works by taking the patient’s own red blood cells, enlarging them externally in a table-top machine to allow them to be perfused with dexamethasone, a prodrug corticosteroid, and reinserting them into the patient. Across dose groups, the only adverse events in the ATTeST trial were mild to moderate and transient, and did not need medical intervention to resolve [page 9].

Like in other indications, corticosteroid use is generally not advised in A-T as it leads to immunodeficiency issues and bone loss. Although its efficacy has been established, traditional administration leads to a temporary peak dose accompanied by toxicity, and the effect of such peak dose quickly tapers. eDSP tackles this issue because its mechanism of action allows for slow release during the course of a month, without toxicity. In that respect, Quince reported that growth and bone mineral density were not negatively affected over 24-month treatment with EryDex.

Efficacy in smaller earlier trials

Quince wishes to expand eDSP to other indications in case of success of the NEAT trial.

Earlier trials showed that this is realistic. Trials with lower doses than the dose currently used in the NEAT trial have been run in ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s disease, cystic fibrosis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, showing reduction or termination of corticosteroid use.

Quince plans to rely on this data using the US 505(b)(2) regulatory pathway for approval of eDSP in A-T.

Valuation potential

Quince plans to file an NDA with the FDA in 2H 2026 in case of good results, and has a strategic partnership with Option Care Health, America’s leading outpatient infusion provider, to that purpose.

The FDA granted eDSP fast-track designation, and both the FDA and the EU’s EMA have granted it orphan drug designation, which should allow for respectively seven and ten years of exclusivity as of the moment of approval in case of approval in the US and the EU.

Rare disease therapies often cost $350,000 - $1million per year. Recently approved drugs for Niemann’s Pick disease could serve as an example. Annual costs of both treatments Miplyffa and Aqneursa are respectively $503,600 - $1,328,000 and $366,350 - $732,650. Many of these drugs are forecast to reach $1 billion or more in peak sales. A good example is Skyclaris, acquired in 2023 by Biogen for $7.3 billion, for a similar-sized patient population, with estimated peak sales of $1 - $1.5 billion. Another is Krystal Biotech’s Vyjuvek, expected to have more than $1 billion in peak sales in a smaller indication than Ataxia-Telangiectasia. KRYS currently has a +$7.3 billion market cap.

From that perspective, I believe there may be substantial room for upside in case of success for A-T alone. The development pipeline for A-T is also rather minimal. Intrabio is furthest advanced, with the drug Aqneursa or levacetylleucine which has been approved for Nieman’s Pick Disease in September 2024, where it competes with Zevra Therapeutics’ (ZVRA) Mypliffa, which had been approved as the first treatment for that indication a week earlier. Aqneursa operates through activation of cerebral glucose metabolism in the cerebellum. From the perspective of its MoA, there does not seem to be a similarity to eDSP. Results from an earlier phase 2 trial failed to reveal significant benefits. Initial results from Intrabio’s Phase 2 trial were not retrieved, but the extension phase of that trial apparently yielded positive results.

Quince mentioned during their recent Investor Day [at 1h.19min.] that, should a competing asset advance to approval, eDSP has the ability to be dosed concomitantly.

eDSP’s platform play potential should furthermore not be underestimated. eDSP may additionally find different applications in several indications, for which the use of corticosteroids is still the standard of care. The first indication the company is looking at is Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD), for which treatment with corticosteroids provides delayed disease progression, but comes with similar toxicity issues. The recently approved Duvyzat is indicated for patients who are already on corticosteroids, after having established a benefit in such patients in a placebo-controlled trial. Capricor Therapeutics recently reported success in DMD as well, but from an allogeneic cell therapy, and its results have been criticized. Quince plans to start dosing its first patients in a Phase 2 study in Duchenne’s in the course of this year.

Finances

Quince had $26.3 million in cash at the end of Q3 2025. In June 2025, Quince completed a financing comprising $11.5 million in upfront proceeds and up to $10.4 million in warrants at premiums to the market price at the time.

Net loss for the Q3 2025 quarter was $13.3 million. Over the first nine months of 2025, net cash used in operating activities was $30.9 million, i.e. $10.3 million on average.

Quince expects cash to be sufficient until into the second quarter of 2026, i.e. after the Phase 3 readout for A-T, and intends to raise more in case of success to fund further activities, including NDA filing and commercialization. Upon a positive readout, such a further financing is common.

Risks

Several risks to a Buy thesis exist here.

Quince’s stock has increased significantly over the past months. The market may correct at least some of that in the near term, but looking at the chart, I believe that correction may already be in place.

Another risk is that placebo may outperform previous historical results.

There is a chance that the Phase 3 NEAT trial that Quince is running does not generate statistically significant results, or that the market does not interpret the results as a success. One could argue that the same mechanism of action has failed to generate statistically significant results in this indication before—primarily due to age-related study design issues of ATTeST discussed earlier.

Even in case of success, the regulatory authorities would still have to approve the drug, and commercialization would still have to be successful afterwards. There is always also a competitive threat, as mentioned above.

Conclusion

Quince Therapeutics is slated to release topline data from the NEAT trial in Ataxia-Telangiectasia in mid-Q1 2026, which I assume will be next month.

I believe several elements speak in favor of potential success here.

First, the safety data seems excellent. There is apparent efficacy of corticosteroids in Ataxia-Telangiectasia, but that efficacy comes with downsides in the case of conventional administration. eDSP seems to solve that, as has been shown in an open-label Phase 2 trial and later Phase 3 ATTeST trial.

The NEAT trial setup deals with apparent trial flaws from the past, such as the inclusion of patients with plateaued progression in the primary analysis population, and Covid-related data quality issues. My analysis of the chances of success as set out above indicates a high probability for the trial to be successful.

There seems to be alignment and support from the regulatory authorities, as shown by the SPA on the Phase 3 trial, fast track designation, and orphan drug designations.

Given the promising setup and high-risk, high-reward prospects, I think a Buy rating is in place here.
